title: What is a package
instruction:
Every Go file starts with `package`. Think of it as the file's team jersey — it tells Go which group this file belongs to.
`package main` is special: it's where `func main()` lives and the program starts.
Run this — it imports `fmt` and `strings`, two built-in packages for printing and text operations.
successMessage: Two imports, two tools. `fmt` for printing, `strings` for cleaning up book titles. Go's standard library has dozens of these built-in packages.

title: Searching and transforming book titles
instruction:
The `strings` package handles almost every text operation a librarian needs.
- `strings.Contains(s, sub)` — does `s` contain `sub`?
- `strings.Count(s, sub)` — how many times does `sub` appear?
- `strings.ReplaceAll(s, old, new)` — replace all occurrences
Add three lines: print whether title contains "Great", count how many times "e" appears, and replace spaces with ` | `.

codeChecks.message: Use strings.Contains(title, "Great") to check if it contains "Great".
Use strings.Count(title, "e") to count occurrences of "e".
Use strings.ReplaceAll to replace spaces with " | ".
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Contains → true. Count → 3 (twice in "Great", once in "Gatsby"). Replace → separated by pipes. The strings package is one of the most used in all of Go.

title: ahha — library statistics with math
instruction:
The `math` package helps you calculate library stats — average pages, shelf space, membership growth.
`math.Sqrt(x)` — square root. `math.Pow(x, y)` — x to the power y. `math.Abs(x)` — absolute value.
Add three lines: print sqrt(144) (total members), 2^10 (book copies after 10 doublings), and abs(-7.5) (fee difference).

codeChecks.message: Print math.Sqrt(144).
Print math.Pow(2, 10).
Print math.Abs(-7.5).
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: √144=12 members, 2¹⁰=1024 book copies, |-7.5|=7.5 fee difference. The math package handles every standard operation.

title: Managing due dates with time
instruction:
The `time` package handles everything a librarian needs: due dates, borrowing periods, and overdue calculations.
`time.Now()` — current date/time. `t.Format("2006-01-02")` — format a date (Go uses a reference date).
Run this. Then add a line that prints today's date in `DD/MM/YYYY` format — like a due-date slip.

codeChecks.message: Use now.Format("02/01/2006") to print in DD/MM/YYYY format.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Go's time formatting is unique — instead of %d %m %Y, you use the reference date Jan 2 15:04:05 2006. Great for printing due-date slips!

title: Exported vs unexported
instruction:
In Go, whether a function or type is public or private is decided by a single rule:
**Capital first letter = exported (usable outside the package)**
**Lowercase first letter = unexported (private to the package)**
Run this. Then fix the code by changing `book` to `Book` and struct fields to start with capitals.
hint: Change book to Book, title to Title, pages to Pages in both the struct definition and usage.

codeChecks.message: Capitalise the type name: type Book struct
Capitalise the field: Title string
Capitalise the field: Pages int
Capitalise the function: func Display(...)

successMessage: One rule, zero keywords. No public, no private, no protected — just the case of the first letter.

title: What is a module and using third-party packages
instruction:
A module is a collection of Go packages with a shared identity and version — like a library's catalog system with multiple departments.
Every Go project has a `go.mod` file. You create one with `go mod init module-name`.
Beyond the standard library, Go's ecosystem has thousands of packages at `pkg.go.dev`.
Here we use `encoding/json` (standard library) the same way you would use any external package. Run this to see JSON output.
successMessage: A Go Book struct became JSON in three lines. This is how every library API serializes catalog data.

title: Import aliases
instruction:
You can give an imported package an alias:
`import ( str "strings"; m "math" )`
Then use `str.ToUpper()` and `m.Sqrt()` instead of the full names.
Run this. Then add a line using the `m` alias to print `math.Pi` rounded to 2 decimal places.

codeChecks.message: Use m.Pi (the math alias) to access Pi.
Format Pi to 2 decimal places with %.2f.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Aliases keep imports readable when names get long or conflict. In a library app with many dependencies, they make your code cleaner.

title: Blank import
instruction:
Sometimes you import a package purely for its side effects — it registers something when it loads, but you never call its functions directly.
`import _ "some/package"` — the blank identifier suppresses the "imported and not used" error.
Common examples: database drivers (registers with `database/sql`), image decoders.
Run this. The `image/png` package is blank-imported to register the PNG decoder.
expectedOutput: PNG support registered via blank import
successMessage: The blank import ran the png package's init() function — registering the PNG decoder — without polluting your code with unused identifiers.

title: Build a library catalog tool
instruction:
No starter code. Build a small library catalog tool using `strings`, `fmt`, and `math`:
1. Start with `"  The Great Gatsby by F. Scott Fitzgerald, 1925  "`
2. Trim whitespace, print in UPPERCASE
3. Split into words and print the word count
4. Print whether it contains `"Gatsby"`
5. Print the average word length rounded to 2 decimal places
hint: For average length: loop through words, sum all len(word), divide by len(words). Use %.2f for formatting.
expectedOutput: THE GREAT GATSBY BY F. SCOTT FITZGERALD, 1925

codeChecks.message: Use strings.TrimSpace to trim the whitespace.
Use strings.ToUpper to print in uppercase.
Use strings.Split to split into words.
Use strings.Contains to check for "Gatsby".
Use math package or %.2f to format the average to 2 decimal places.

successMessage: Five packages worth of functionality in under 20 lines. Entry trimmed, uppercased, split, searched, and analysed. The standard library does the heavy lifting.
